normalized_obligations.nl_gloss: No explicit preconditions are required by the implementation; it works for any input lists (including empty lists).

normalized_obligations.lean_statement:
let m := a.length; let n := b.length; ∃ (dp : Fin (m+1) → Fin (n+1) → Nat),
  (∀ i : Fin (m+1), dp i 0 = 0) ∧ (∀ j : Fin (n+1), dp 0 j = 0) ∧
  (∀ i : Fin m, ∀ j : Fin n,
    dp (Fin.succ i) (Fin.succ j) =
      if a.get ⟨i.1, by simp⟩ = b.get ⟨j.1, by simp⟩
      then dp i j + 1
      else Nat.max (dp i (Fin.succ j)) (dp (Fin.succ i) j))
normalized_obligations.nl_gloss: The DP table is initialized with zeros on the first row and first column, and filled by the standard LCS recurrence using the equality of elements at positions i-1 and j-1.
All DP accesses dp[i][j] are within bounds for i ∈ [0..m] and j ∈ [0..n].

normalized_obligations.lean_statement:
let m := a.length; let n := b.length;
∀ i : Fin (m+1),
  (i.1 = 0 → True) ∧
  (i.1 = 0 → dp i ⟨0, by simp⟩ = 0) ∧
∀ j : Fin (n+1),
  (j.1 = 0 → True) ∧
  (j.1 = 0 → dp ⟨0, by simp⟩ j = 0)
normalized_obligations.nl_gloss: The first row and first column are set to 0, matching the fact that the LCS length with an empty prefix is 0.
When the current elements match, the DP increases the diagonal value by 1; otherwise it takes the maximum of skipping one element from either sequence.
Introduce the intended interpretation of dp[i][j] as the LCS length of prefixes a[0..i) and b[0..j). (This obligation is a placeholder for connecting dp to the standard LCS-over-prefixes definition.)
For all i,j, the filled DP value equals the length of the longest common subsequence of the corresponding prefixes.

normalized_obligations.lean_statement:
let m := a.length; let n := b.length;
  dp ⟨m, by simp⟩ ⟨n, by simp⟩ = lcsLen a b
normalized_obligations.nl_gloss: The function returns dp[m][n], which equals the LCS length of the full input lists.
